mnemonics.title: SINGLE USER - ONE PLACE
mnemonics.content: Single-Level Directory: All files stored in ONE central location. Like a single drawer where all citizens keep their files - simple but confusing when files have same name.

mnemonics.title: TWO TIERS - USER SEPARATES
mnemonics.content: Two-Level Directory: Each user gets their OWN subdirectory under root. Users can't see each other's files directly - like separate lockers for each student in a school.

mnemonics.title: HIERARCHY - BRANCHES EXPAND
mnemonics.content: Hierarchical Directory: Tree-like structure with root at top, branches below. Files organized in folders within folders - like a family tree or organizational chart.

mnemonics.title: TREE - NO CYCLES GLAD
mnemonics.content: Tree Directory: Each directory has ONE parent (except root). NO cycles allowed - like a real tree growing upward, branches never connect back to trunk.

mnemonics.title: ACYCLIC - NO BACK LINKS
mnemonics.content: Acyclic Graph Directory: Allows SHARED subdirectories and files (shortcuts/links) but NO cycles - like multiple people sharing a Google Doc, changes reflect for all.

mnemonics.title: GENERAL GRAPH - DANGEROUS LOOPS
mnemonics.content: General Graph Directory: Allows cycles (loops) - dangerous as it can cause infinite loops during traversal. Like a circular road with no exit.

mnemonics.title: PATH - PERMANENT ADDRESS
mnemonics.content: Absolute Path: Complete path from ROOT directory to file. Like full home address from country level. Always starts from root (/).

mnemonics.title: RELATIVE - FROM WHERE YOU ARE
mnemonics.content: Relative Path: Path from CURRENT working directory. Like directions from your current location (e.g., 'go 2 blocks north').

acronyms.usage: The directory you are currently in. Use 'pwd' command in Linux to see your CWD. All relative paths start from here.
Your personal directory where you start when you log in. In Linux, represented by '~'. Default location for personal files.
List of directories where OS searches for executable files. Typing 'python' works because python's location is in PATH.
Linux command to display current directory path. Essential for understanding where you are in directory structure.
Command to navigate between directories. 'cd ..' goes to parent directory, 'cd ~' goes to home.
Command to list contents of directory. 'ls -l' shows detailed listing with permissions, size, date modified.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted tree with root at the TOP (not bottom). Root is the trunk - everything branches DOWN from it. Symbol: '/'
Think of an organizational chart - CEO at top (root), managers below, employees at bottom. Information flows down, commands go up.
Family tree orientation - parent directory is ABOVE, child directory is BELOW. Parent contains children, like a folder containing subfolders.
Absolute = GPS coordinates (always same). Relative = 'Turn left from here' (changes based on current location). Root (/) is your reference point for absolute.
Think of it as a 'shortcut' or 'alias' - like a signpost pointing to another location. The link appears in directory but doesn't contain actual data.
Imagine walking through a building - going 'cd ..' means going up one floor, 'cd foldername' means entering a room on current floor.

analogies.analogy: A single classroom where ALL students must keep their files in one common pile - chaos if two students name files same, impossible to organize.
Each student gets their OWN locker - students can't see inside others' lockers, but all lockers are in same corridor (root).
University with colleges (faculties), then departments, then year folders, then individual student files - organized, logical structure.
A shared Google Doc - multiple people access same document, changes reflect for all, but document exists in only one location.
A circular reference in Excel - A points to B, B points to C, C points back to A. Following it leads to infinite loop, hard to find starting point.
Your current location in a library - you're in the 'Computer Science' aisle, so looking for a book here is faster than going to main entrance first.
Finding a friend's house - Absolute path is using full address with city, state, country. Relative path is 'turn left at the red house' from where you currently stand.
